coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped biomass derived carbon dots as a fluorescence dual-mode sensing platform for detection of tetracyclines in biological and food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Yanchun
Cheng, Zhe
Wang, Guanghui
Shuang, Shaomin
Zhou, Yehong
Dong, Chuan
Du, Fangfang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized hydrothermally using abundantly accessible pitaya peel and 1,2-ethylenediamine as precursors. N-CDs exhibited favorable photostability, which can serve as a multifunctional nano-sensor for detection of three tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) based on fluorescence (FL) dual-mode sensing strategy. The FL intensity of N-CDs could be rapidly quenched by tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) based on bandgap transition, inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching (SQ) and electrostatic interaction. While a new finding that FL of N-CDs demonstrated a remarkable enhancement in the presence of chlortetracycline (CTC) with the same detection mechanisms as TC and OTC, also including the aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Furthermore, an easily extensible fluorescence sensor array was developed based on multiple CDs for identifying multiple TCs in real samples. Therefore, the constructed N-CDs provides a new perspective for choosing extensive natural biomass to synthesize CDs, further developing a novel sensor to realize their versatile sensing application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tetracycline antibiotics
Dual-mode sensing
Inner filter effect
Aggregation induced emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of TC in real samples
Detection of TCs based on N-CDs
Determination of TCs in real samples
Fluorescence pattern identification of TCs by a CDs sensor array
Discrimination of TCs in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) size distribution of N-CDs.
(A) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs, (B) XPS survey scan, (C) C1 s XPS, (D) N1 s XPS and (E) O1 s XPS.
(A) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of the N-CDs; (B) Emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of TCs (A) TC, (B) OTC and (C) CTC. Inset: The fluorescence intensities ratio of N-CDs as a function of TCs concentration.

(A) The overlap between the absorption spectra of TC, CTC and OTC and the excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (B) Fluorescence decay of N-CDs in the absence and presence of TC, CTO and CTC as a function of time at a λex/λem of 381/456 nm.
The influence of interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.
Two-dimensional PCA score plot of fluorescence sensor array for recognition of mixtures of OTC/CTC and TC/CTC at different molar ratios (total concentration at 40 μM).
Two-dimensional PCA score plot for identification of three TCs with the same concentration at 40 μM in the real samples.
Schematic illustration of synthetic process of N-CDs and application as a multifunctional nano-sensor for sensing of TC, OTC and CTC.
Analytical results for the detection of TCs in real samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped biomass derived carbon dots as a fluorescence dual-mode sensing platform for detection of tetracyclines in biological and food samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized hydrothermally using abundantly accessible pitaya peel and 1,2-ethylenediamine as precursors. N-CDs exhibited favorable photostability, which can serve as a multifunctional nano-sensor for detection of three tetracycline antibiotics (TCs) based on fluorescence (FL) dual-mode sensing strategy. The FL intensity of N-CDs could be rapidly quenched by tetracycline (TC) and oxytetracycline (OTC) based on bandgap transition, inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching (SQ) and electrostatic interaction. While a new finding that FL of N-CDs demonstrated a remarkable enhancement in the presence of chlortetracycline (CTC) with the same detection mechanisms as TC and OTC, also including the aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Furthermore, an easily extensible fluorescence sensor array was developed based on multiple CDs for identifying multiple TCs in real samples. Therefore, the constructed N-CDs provides a new perspective for choosing extensive natural biomass to synthesize CDs, further developing a novel sensor to realize their versatile sensing application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), a class of significant and wide-spectrum antibiotics, their applications as veterinary drugs in animal husbandry and the therapy of human and animal infections have been generally satisfactory. Nevertheless, the overuse of TCs could result in undesirable drug-resistant infections, which is the potential public health threat all over the world (Huang et al., 2018). While their residues of TCs have been detected in meat and other products of animal origin, which may lead to allergic reactions in sensitized individuals (Cla et al., 2020; Xu, Zhang, Jia, Bi, & Zhao, 2019). To ensure food security, European Union has announced maximum residue limits (MRLs) of 225 nM for TCs in milk (Sczesny, Nau, & Hamscher, 2003). In addition, China’s Ministry of agriculture (CMA) also has established the MRLs of the above three TCs in muscle tissue of animal foods, milk, fish are 200 g/kg, 100 g/kg, and 200 g/kg, respectively (China, 2019). The residue level of TCs over MRLs in animal products cause serious harm to human health (Lin et al., 2017). Therefore, it is meaningful and highly anticipated for developing sensitive approach toward the detection of TCs residues.

To date, numerous analysis methods have been developed for TCs detection, such as chemiluminescence (CE) (Sun, Dai, Zhu, Han, & Luo, 2020), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Mookantsa, Dube, & Nindi, 2016), electrochemistry (Masawat & Slater, 2007), colorimetry (Wang et al., 2016) and mass spectrometry (MS) (Lara et al., 2006). Although most of them have satisfactory sensitivity, some of them have several inherent drawbacks such as sophisticated instruments, long time-consuming, professional operations, and poor reproducibility, which limited applications. In contrast, fluorescence method as a powerful tool, has been widely used in analysis in virtue of its simplicity, nondestructive nature, and high sensitivity (Lu et al., 2016). Fluorescence-based probes have been developed to monitor TCs for clinical diagnosis. For instance, Yu et al. (Yu et al., 2019) designed a metal-organic framework of pyromellitic acid (Zn-BTEC) as a platform for the selective identification of CTC by the enhance aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Leng et al. (Leng, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2013) developed a Zn-based metal–organic coordination polymers for discriminable detection of TCs. Lin et al. (Lin & Wang, 2015) prepared silicon nanoparticles for the determination of TCs by dramatically quenching fluorescence. However, some of these probes usually were subjected to complex preparation process, heavy metal toxicity, high cost. Therefore, it is imperative to develop nanoprobes with simpler preparation, low cost, excellent luminescence performance, and environmental friendliness for the efficient TCs detection.

Since carbon dots (CDs) were discovered by Xu et al. in 2004, as an emerging star of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures (Xu et al., 2004), have gained considerable attention in sensing, bioimaging, optoelectronics, nanomedicine, energy storage/conversion in virtue of their ultrasmall size (<10 nm) (Kim et al., 2018), excellent biocompatibility, intrinsic low toxicity, and photostability (Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020; Mondal et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2019; Zhang, Wang, Yu, & Li, 2020). CDs are commonly comprised of C, H, and O, and their luminescence performance is not good enough to applications (Hou, Yan, Zhao, Li, Ding, & Ding, 2013). Heteroatom doping by the introduction of dopants could meaningfully improve the photoluminescence behavior of CDs, such as high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and new surface state (Kitchawengkul et al., 2021; Miao, Liang, Zhu, Yang, & Kong, 2020; Yang et al., 2012). N doping is one of the most common approaches to advance properties (Ayub, Mohammad, Nasrin, & La, 2018). N-CDs with various FL behavior have also been established and extensively used for the determination of analytes such as ions, molecules, and others (Kaur, Kaur, & Sharma, 2018). In recent years, preparation of CDs using food waste as a precursor has aroused the curiosity of the scientific community due to their excellent performance and benefits to economic or environment, such as the low cost and easy availability of raw materials, superficially abundant functional groups, simplicity of preparation, and environmental friendliness. The research on the applications of N-CDs from biomass is still under active exploration. Therefore, it is worthwhile to introduce facile and cost-effective techniques for enrichment of N-CDs preparation and application.

Herein, we proposed a multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform via using FL signal “turn-off/on” strategy for the detection of different TCs including CTC, OTC, and TC (Scheme 1 ). The as-prepared N-CDs were prepared through one-step hydrothermal synthesis route using pitaya peel and 1,2-ethylenediamine as the precursors. N-CDs exhibited fascinating optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. The fluorescence signal of N-CDs was greatly changed by TCs owing to the IFE, bandgap transition, SQ and electrostatic interaction, while the CTC effectively enhanced FL signal of N-CDs was also due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Therefore, N-CDs as label-free nanoprobes were capable of detecting three kinds of TCs through different responses to fluorescent signals with high sensitivity. In addition, the fluorescence sensor array was extended to distinguish TCs in real samples.

Tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), other antibiotics and 1,2-ethylenediamine were from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of AR grade, which were used without further purification. The ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q Plus system.

N-CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method with pitaya peel and 1,2-ethylenediamine as raw materials. Fresh pitaya peel was crushed with a juicer and frozen for later use. Then, 0.5 g of pitaya peel was put into a 100 mL beaker. Then, 8 mL ultrapure water and 8 mL of 1,2-ethylenediamine were added successively. After mixing evenly, the mixture was reacted in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 150 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling to ambient temperatures, N-CDs solution firstly filtered with filter paper to remove large particle impurities, and then dialysis was performed using dialysis membrane (500–1000 Da) for 12 h to remove other small molecules. The above N-CDs aqueous solution was freeze-dried to obtain a dark brown N-CDs solid.

The stock solution (5 mg/mL) of N-CDs was firstly prepared by taking appropriate amounts of N-CDs solid and diluting it with ultrapure water. 500 μL of above stock solution of N-CDs was diluted in 2 mL of PBS buffer solution to measure TCs concentration. Then, different concentrations of TCs standard solutions were added to the above solution. FL response of OTC and TC were measured when incubated for 5 and 7 min, while the FL response of CTC was measured after 50 min of incubation. The FL spectra were recorded from 400 to 700 nm upon excitation light at 381 nm. For the evaluation of selectivity of N-CDs, it is necessary to replace TCs with other similar substances under the identical experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of TC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Honey, milk, fish and pork were selected as the actual samples. Honey and milk samples were purchased from Lingde supermarket of Shanxi University. Fresh pork and fish were bought at Xuxi farmers' market from Xiaodian District, Taiyuan. Preliminary treatment of milk and honey samples were based on our previous reports (Du, Cheng, Wang, Li, & Dong, 2021). Pork and skinless fish were crushed respectively with a meat grinder, then 0.5 g of minced meat was put into an aqueous solution (5 mL) containing trichloroacetic acid (50 μL). After being homogenized for 5 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The pH of supernatant was adjusted to pH = 7 by adding NaOH (1 M), then filtrated with 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the supernatant was diluted to100 mL. The standard TCs solution was spiked into the prepared supernatant of above sample treated for further analysis.

The optimization preparation conditions of N-CDs were described in detail (Table S1 and Table S2). The morphology and size of N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images and histogram of granulometric distribution verified the formation of quasi-spherical N-CDs with an average particle size of 9.5 nm (Fig. 1 B). The inset of Fig. 1A proved that N-CDs had lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, suggesting that the synthesized N-CDs have a crystal structure and agree with previous works about N-CDs (Su, Han, Yan, Jin, & Lu, 2020). From Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 2 A), the absorption band around 3307 cm−1 was account for stretching vibration of OH or NH (Majumdar, Thakur, & Chowdhury, 2020). The weak absorption peak at 2118 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of CH, and stretching vibration peaks at 1563, 1458, 1297 and 1145 cm−1 appear to be related to CC, CN, CO and C-NH-C (Du et al., 2021), respectively. The results show that the surface of the as-synthesized N-CDs contains abundant functional groups such as amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups.

The elemental analysis shows that N-CDs is composed of C 43.75 %, H 9.42 %, N 21.7 %, K 7.31 % (K contained in N-CDs comes from the raw material of pitaya peel) and O 17.82 % (Table S3), demonstrating that N-doped CDs were synthesized successfully. The chemical composition on the surface of N-CDs was further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The three main peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s were found in the full survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B). As shown in Fig. 2C, the high-resolution C 1 s/K 2p can be divided into five peaks, which was attributed to CC (284.45 eV), CN (285.31 eV), CO (287.6 eV), O-COO (292.41 eV) and KOH (295.19 eV) (Bello et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2021). The two peaks in high-resolution N 1 s (Fig. 2D) could be decomposed into pyridine N (398.6 eV) and pyrrole N (399.4 eV), respectively (Mei et al., 2019). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) shows two peaks at 530.76 eV and 531.9 eV, corresponding to CO and C-OH/COC, respectively (Huo et al., 2019). The XPS spectra analysis is consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis.

The optical characteristics of N-CDs including UV–vis absorption, FL spectrum, FL lifetime and photostability were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, an obvious absorption peak at 286 nm was observed, which may be due to π-π* transition of CC of N-CDs, and the weak adsorption peak around 350 nm may be originated from the n-π* transition of CO. The transparent N-CDs solutions exhibit strong blue FL with a QY of 5.6 %. Under different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm, fluorescence emission peaks were red-shifted (Fig. 3B), which was attributed to the quantum effect and different emissive trap sites on the surface of N-CDs (Li et al., 2021; Wareing, Gentile, & Phan, 2021).

The effects of different factors on the photostability of N-CDs were investigated. For the salt tolerance of N-CDs, the FL intensity hardly changed with the incremental concentration of NaCl from 0 to 3 M (Fig. S1A), indicating that N-CDs have great application potential in physiological salt environment. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. S1B, N-CDs showed excellent photobleaching resistance after prolonged exposure under 365 nm UV lamp for 90 min with 15 min durations for each time. The above results demonstrate that the prepared N-CDs have favorable photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs based on N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: OTC, TC, and CTC were taken as representative analytes for detecting TCs. The effect of pH on FL intensity of N-CDs was explored for monitoring TCs (Fig. S1C). The FL intensity of N-CDs remained stable in the pH range from 2.0 to 12.0. Considering the biological application, pH 7.0 was used in subsequent experiments. The response time of TCs detection system was also investigated. As seen from fluorescence curve (Fig. S2A), the FL intensity of N-CDs could be rapidly quenched by TC and OTC after incubation for 5 and 7 mins. FL of N-CDs in the presence of CTC, was recorded for 63 mins with 3 mins durations for each time, and their FL continuously increased and then retained after 50 mins (Fig. S2B). As a result, the optimal response time for CTC detection was determined to be 50 mins for the following measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the FL of the N-CDs gradually declined with the increasing of concentration of TC and OTC, while only CTC induced FL enhancement of N-CDs. The FL intensity ratios of F0/F versus TC and OTC concentrations and F/F0 versus CTC concentration both displayed good relationships in the range of 0.2–56.0 μM for TC, 0.4–100.0 μM for OTC and 0.4–100.0 μM for CTC, with the limit of detection (LOD) values of 33.8, 40.5 and 41.9 nM（(S/N = 3), respectively, which was lower than the acceptable MRLs by food safety criterion of European Union (Lu, Jiang, Li, Chen, & Wong, 2004). In comparison with other FL methods for the TCs detection, the performances of sensing system were also comparable with great majority of reported FL probes (Table S4). The results indicated that N-CDs were capable of using as a multifunctional sensing platform for the determination of TCs through fluorescence “turn off” and “turn on” modes.

The possible sensing mechanism of N-CDs for TCs was researched through FL, UV–vis absorption, FL lifetime, and ζ potential. TCs were almost non-fluorescent compared with FL intensity of N-CDs under the same experimental conditions (Fig. S3). FL emission wavelengths of N-CDs were red-shifted after addition of TC and OTC, and blue-shifted as CTC was introduced (Fig. 4), which were caused by the band gap transitions. From Fig. 5 A and Table S5, the FL lifetimes of N-CDs before and after addition of TC, OTC, and CTC were measured and determined to be 9.334, 9.297, 9.247 and 9.224 ns, respectively. The FL lifetimes of N-CDs were hardly variable in the presence of TCs. Therefore, the fluorescence changes may occur either by inner filter effect (IFE) or static quenching (SQ) for TCs detection. Fig. 5B indicated that excitation band of N-CDs and absorption peak of TCs have an overlap, suggesting the presence of IFE for TCs sensing. In addition, Fig. S4 showed a red shift in the maximum absorption peaks of TC and OTC after the mixture of N-CDs/TC or N-CDs/OTC, while the absorption peak of CTC had a blue-shift from 371 nm to 345 nm, which proved that N-CDs with TCs formed a stable complex and SQ also had a contribution for the TCs detection. As described in Fig. S5, the zeta potentials of N-CDs varied from −31.8 to −14.8, −21.1 and −15.4 mV with the addition of TC, OTC and CTC, respectively, indicating that TCs tended to consume the carboxyl or other negative groups on surface of N-CDs by electrostatic interaction. TC and OTC have a similar structure to CTC (Fig. S6). However, only CTC could enhance the fluorescence of N-CDs. DLS experiments revealed mean sizes of N-CDs were 14.8 nm, 14.1 nm, 11.7 nm and 24.8 nm before and after the addition of TC, OTC and CTC, respectively, which only demonstrated aggregation between N-CDs and CTC. The reason is that CTC embraces a chlorine atom, which is able to result in the p-π conjugate interaction with N-CDs, and thus induce the aggregation of N-CDs. Furthermore, the coordination between potassium atoms of N-CDs and CTC molecules use π-π stacking to form π-conjugated six-member rings, which stimulated the aggregate of CTC (Yang et al., 2019). Therefore, the bandgap transition, IFE, SQ and electrostatic interaction were proposed to be the detection mechanisms for TCs, moreover CTC induced the aggregation of N-CDs to enhance FL, especially the p-π conjugate interaction between them (Li, Hou, Yuan, Long, & Chen, 2020).

Selectivity of N-CDs as a label-free nanoprobe was evaluated by examining the effects of potential interfering substances including metal ions (K+, Ca2+ and Al3+), anions (SO3 2-, NO3 – and CO3 2–), antibiotics (thiamphenicol, kanamycin, gentamicin, penicillin, TC, OTC, CTC and AZM), amino acids (l-Cys and l-Lys) and GSH on the emission intensity of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , a significant FL quenching effect for TC and OTC, and an enhancement effect for CTC were observed, while other interfering substances displayed a negligible effect on the FL intensity of N-CDs. The results showed that the N-CDs could be an effective versatile dual-mode sensing platform for the detection of TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the feasibility, the proposed method was validated by application to the detection of the three TCs in food samples. The various concentrations of TCs standard solutions were added to pre-treated honey, milk, fish and pork samples for the determination according to above method. Each concentration was measured in parallel six times, and the results were listed in Table 1 . The recoveries of TCs in food samples were obtained from 90.1 % to 101.3 % with RSD from 1.07 % to 3.67 %. These observations provide a high potential for further use in food safety analysis and biological sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence pattern identification of TCs by a CDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: A quadruple-channel fluorescence (FL) sensor array based on two kinds of CDs was fabricated to detect and discriminate TCs. The FL of N-CDs and g-CDs at 456 and 519 nm (defined S1 and S4) were employed as two independent sensor elements, FL of the mixture (N-CDs and g-CDs, m-CDs) can be referred to as the two auxiliary elements (defined as S2 and S3) at 460 and 495 nm under 390 nm UV light to improve the recognition ability for TCs (Fig. S7A). The fluorescence of g-CDs and m-CDs were quenched by TCs (Fig. S7B and Fig. S7C). These differentiated FL responses (F0-F)/F0 in S1, S2, S3 and S4 were analyzed by principal component analysis (PCA) to generate a specific fingerprint map, and a FL response matrix (6 replicates × 3 TCs × 4 FL intensity variation rate) were built to gain two canonical factors (PC1, 70.75 %; PC2, 29.06 %) (Fig. S8), in which each TC was distributed in different locations at 95 % confidence. These results show that the fluorescence sensor array has great potential to achieve accurate discrimination of TCs. Furthermore, the sensitivity of fluorescence sensor array toward TCs was researched separately. Fig. S9A,C,E, display the score plots of CDs against three TCs at various concentrations (1–100 μM) at 85 % confidence. As illustrated Fig. S9B, D, and F, the canonical factor PC1 of each TC followed a linear distribution pattern at different concentrations in the score maps, and linear detection range for TC, OTC, and CTC was evaluated as 1–60 μM, 1–40 μM, 1–80 μM, respectively.

To further differentiate mixed samples, the various molar ratios of binary mixtures of OTC/CTC (0:1, 1:4, 2:3, 3:2, 4:1, 1:0) and TC/OTC (1:4, 2:3, 3:2, 4:1, 1:0) at 40 μM was randomly set to assess identification capability of system. Fig. 7 showed the distributions of all eleven mixture samples and the samples were clearly separated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the applicability in real samples, three TCs spiked in the treated honey, milk, fish and pork samples were determined by this sensor array. As can be seen from Fig. 8 , three TCs were categorized into three different groups in the food samples without overlap and blank real samples are independent to TCs in PCA score plot. In addition, relative locations of three TCs in real samples are similar with its in PBS solution (pH = 7.4). It is suggested that this proposed sensor array has potentials to identify TCs in real samples.

In summary, a facile hydrothermal approach was used to prepare N-CDs by carbonizing readily available natural biomass and 1,2-ethylenediamine. The elaborate spectral study offered evidence that N-CDs could be capable of a dual-mode FL nano-sensor for quantitative analysis of TCs. In the presence of TC and OTC, the fluorescent signal of N-CDs could be decreased rapidly and efficiently as a “turn off” sensor. However, the interaction between CTC and N-CDs enhances FL, which enables N-CDs to be a pioneering “turn-on” sensor for highly sensitive detection of CTC. Gaining from their novel optical performance, N-CDs can be applied to detect TCs by a dual-mode sensing strategy, and a fluorescent array sensor by two kinds of CDs was fabricated to recognize TCs in real samples, which provides a great potential for application in biological analysis and food safety.

Yanchun Jia: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhe Cheng: Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Guanghui Wang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Shaomin Shuang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yehong Zhou: Resources. Chuan Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Fangfang Du: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134245.
